finance-markets.title: The end of cheap
finance-markets.summary: For the last 50 years, the cost of everything—from factory equipment to workers' salaries to electricity—has been getting cheaper, making life easier for American businesses and consumers. But economists are warning that this era is ending. Rising wages, more expensive energy, and higher costs to build factories mean that inflation (when prices go up overall) could become a bigger problem going forward. This matters to you because it could mean higher prices at stores, higher rent, and possibly fewer job opportunities. The Federal Reserve (America's central bank that controls interest rates) will have to carefully manage this shift to avoid economic problems like recessions (when the economy shrinks).

finance-markets.title: Wall Street bulls bet US stocks rally will defy bubble fears
finance-markets.summary: Investors on Wall Street (the financial district in New York where stocks are bought and sold) are betting that U.S. stock prices will keep climbing despite warnings that the market might be overheating—meaning prices have risen so fast they might crash. The main reason for optimism is artificial intelligence (AI), which many believe will transform how companies make money. However, critics worry that AI stocks have become overvalued, meaning people are paying too much for them compared to their actual worth. Think of it like buying baseball cards: if everyone suddenly wants them and prices skyrocket, they might become too expensive and the bubble could pop. If the market crashes, it could affect your family's savings and retirement accounts, so understanding these risks matters to everyone.

finance-markets.title: Jay Powell warns Federal Reserve is undergoing 'stress test'
finance-markets.summary: Jay Powell, who used to lead the Federal Reserve (America's central bank that controls interest rates and money supply), is warning that the institution faces serious challenges from political pressure. Donald Trump has suggested he wants to fire Federal Reserve leaders who don't follow his instructions, which Powell argues threatens the rule of law and the Fed's independence. The Federal Reserve is supposed to make decisions based on what's best for the economy, not politics, which is why its independence matters. If politicians can fire Fed leaders whenever they disagree, the Fed might make bad decisions that hurt the economy. This is important because Fed decisions directly affect interest rates on loans, savings accounts, and investments—basically how much money costs for everyone.

finance-markets.title: Berkshire Hathaway to Buy Taylor Morrison for $6.8 Billion
finance-markets.summary: Berkshire Hathaway, a massive investment company run by billionaire Warren Buffett, is buying Taylor Morrison, one of America's biggest homebuilders, for $6.8 billion in an all-cash deal. This is the first major acquisition by Greg Abel, Berkshire's new CEO (chief executive officer). The purchase signals that Berkshire is betting the U.S. housing market will recover and grow in the coming years. Real estate deals this big matter because housing is crucial to the economy—when homebuilding is strong, it creates jobs, increases consumer spending, and boosts economic growth. This deal suggests that even savvy investors believe America's economy and housing sector are headed in a positive direction, though it also means developers are confident they can charge more for houses.

finance-markets.title: Stocks Rise on AI, Oil Climbs as Iran Deal Elusive: Markets Wrap
finance-markets.summary: Stock markets in Asia hit all-time highs as investors continue betting heavily on artificial intelligence companies and technology stocks. At the same time, oil prices rose because a potential peace deal (ceasefire) between the United States and Iran seems unlikely to happen soon. When Middle East tensions increase, oil supplies become uncertain, so traders buy oil as insurance in case prices spike. This matters to you because oil prices affect gas prices at the pump, which influences inflation and how much your family spends on transportation. The strong performance of AI stocks shows global investors remain optimistic about technology's future, but the geopolitical tension (conflicts between countries) reminds us that real-world events can shake financial markets unexpectedly.

finance-markets.title: China's factory activity beats forecasts in May, private survey shows
finance-markets.summary: China's manufacturing sector performed better than expected in May, according to a private survey, suggesting the world's second-largest economy is showing signs of recovery despite some weaker official government data. Manufacturing is crucial because it's the backbone of China's economy and also affects global supply chains—when Chinese factories produce more goods, companies worldwide get the materials they need to make their own products. The fact that private surveys show better results than government statistics raises questions about how accurate China's official numbers are, which matters for investors trying to understand the true health of the global economy. If China's economy strengthens, it could mean more jobs for workers worldwide and cheaper prices for consumers, but it could also mean more competition for American businesses.

finance-markets.title: China's Shoppers Are Buying Luxury Again as Stock Market Rebounds
finance-markets.summary: Chinese consumers are starting to buy more luxury items like high-end fashion and beauty products again, which is a positive sign for global luxury brands that depend heavily on Chinese customers. For years, Chinese shoppers have been hesitant to spend money on expensive products, forcing luxury companies to offer big discounts (which cuts into their profits). This renewed spending is tied to the Chinese stock market's recent strength, meaning as people's investments gain value, they feel wealthier and more confident to spend. This matters globally because China represents an enormous market—if Chinese consumers aren't buying, luxury brands and high-end retailers worldwide suffer. The recovery suggests confidence is returning to China's economy, which could benefit many international companies that sell premium products.

finance-markets.title: India Rate Risks, Fiscal Worries Add to Pressure on Bond Yields
finance-markets.summary: India's bond yields (the interest rates that investors demand when lending money to the Indian government) are rising because investors worry about two things: the possibility of interest rate hikes by India's central bank, and concerns about the government's fiscal position (whether it's spending more money than it takes in). When bond yields rise, it becomes more expensive for India's government and businesses to borrow money, which can slow economic growth. This matters because India is one of the world's fastest-growing major economies, so any slowdown affects global growth. For regular people, higher borrowing costs mean more expensive mortgages, car loans, and business loans, making it harder to invest in the future. Investors worldwide watch India closely because its economy is increasingly important to global commerce and supply chains.

finance-markets.title: China Investors Turn Sellers of HK Stocks in Rare Reversal
finance-markets.summary: Chinese mainland investors have become net sellers of Hong Kong stocks for the first time in nearly three years, meaning they're selling more Hong Kong shares than they're buying. This dramatic reversal happened because technology stocks trading on China's mainland exchanges have surged in value, making them more attractive than Hong Kong stocks. Hong Kong is a major global financial center where companies list shares (go public), and it's especially important for international investors to access Asian companies. When Chinese investors lose interest in Hong Kong stocks, it signals potential weakness in that market and suggests investors believe mainland Chinese tech stocks offer better opportunities. This matters because it affects how money flows through Asia's financial system and could impact international companies trying to raise money in Hong Kong.

finance-markets.title: TSMC's Local Investors Narrow Valuation Gap With Wall Street
finance-markets.summary: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC), the world's largest chipmaker that supplies processors to Apple, Nvidia, and other tech giants, is seeing its U.S.-listed and Taiwan-listed shares trading at more similar prices. Taiwanese investors are heavily buying TSMC stock in Taipei because they believe the AI boom (explosive growth in artificial intelligence demand) will continue driving chip sales higher. TSMC is crucial to understand because it makes the chips inside almost every modern device—smartphones, computers, servers—so its performance affects the entire tech industry. When AI companies need more computing power, they need more chips from TSMC, which makes it a key beneficiary of AI's growth. This stock performance matters because TSMC's success indicates strong confidence in continued AI investment and technology sector growth globally.

world-news.title: Israel seizes castle in Lebanon as it expands ground offensive
world-news.summary: Israel's military, led by Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, has captured a strategic fortress in Lebanon as part of its ongoing campaign against Hezbollah, a militant group that has launched attacks from Lebanese territory. Netanyahu describes this move as a "decisive shift" in the conflict. This escalation is alarming European governments and the international community because it represents Israel expanding its military operations beyond its borders into another country's territory. The situation matters globally because the Middle East is a crucial region for world stability, oil supplies, and international peace. When major powers expand military operations, it can trigger wider regional conflicts and draw in other countries like Iran, the US, and European nations into a larger war.

world-news.title: Iran war live: Israel's expanding invasion of Lebanon draws global alarm
world-news.summary: Israel's military invasion of Lebanon is escalating tensions across the Middle East, prompting France to request an emergency United Nations Security Council meeting to address the situation. The conflict involves Israel fighting Hezbollah, an Iranian-backed militant group operating from Lebanese territory, while Iran continues diplomatic talks with the United States about nuclear agreements. This matters because Lebanon is a fragile country caught between powerful forces, and Israeli military expansion there could destabilize the entire region. If the situation spirals into a wider war involving Iran, it could disrupt global oil markets, affect economies worldwide, and potentially draw in other major powers. The UN Security Council is the body responsible for maintaining international peace and can authorize military action or sanctions.

world-news.title: Trump seeking edits to US-Iran deal, US media report
world-news.summary: Former US President Donald Trump is pushing to renegotiate parts of a nuclear agreement between the United States and Iran, specifically regarding the Strait of Hormuz (a critical shipping channel) and the removal of highly enriched uranium (a material used in nuclear weapons). This matters because the US-Iran relationship affects global stability and oil supplies—Iran controls one of the world's most important waterways for shipping. If Trump successfully changes the deal, it could either reduce nuclear tensions or create new conflicts depending on what terms Iran accepts. The original deal, made in 2015, was designed to prevent Iran from building nuclear weapons. Any changes could signal a shift in US foreign policy and influence how other countries negotiate with Iran.

world-news.title: Ethiopia to vote in first elections since Tigray peace deal
world-news.summary: Ethiopia is holding general elections for the first time since the end of its devastating civil war in the Tigray region, which lasted from 2020 to 2022 and killed hundreds of thousands of people. These elections are significant because they mark Ethiopia's attempt to rebuild and move toward democracy after years of brutal conflict that displaced millions. The vote shows whether the government under Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed can stabilize the country and whether Ethiopians trust the political system. Ethiopia is Africa's second-most populous nation with over 120 million people, so its political direction affects the entire continent. Free and fair elections are crucial for helping citizens have a voice in their government and for the country's long-term peace and development.

world-news.title: Colombia presidential runoff pits leftist senator against pro-Trump rival
world-news.summary: Colombia's presidential election is heading to a runoff on June 21 between left-wing senator Iván Cepeda and far-right outsider Abelardo de la Espriella, who admires former US President Donald Trump. This election matters because Colombia is South America's second-largest economy and a key US ally in Latin America, influencing everything from drug policy to trade. The clash between a leftist and Trump-supporting candidate reflects a global trend where voters are choosing between traditional left and right politics. Colombia faces major challenges including gang violence, drug trafficking, and poverty, so whoever wins will shape the country's direction on security and economic policy. A Trump-aligned leader could shift Colombia's relationship with the US, while a leftist victory could align it more with other left-leaning Latin American governments.

world-news.title: Japan defence minister denies militarism and criticises China's 'huge arsenal'
world-news.summary: Japan's Defence Minister Shinjiro Koizumi is publicly criticizing China's massive military buildup while defending Japan's own military expansion as necessary for self-defense. This is significant because Japan's constitution historically restricted its military after World War II, but China's growing military power is pushing Japan to rearm. Koizumi's pointed remarks reflect rising tensions in Asia, where China's military growth is reshaping the regional balance of power. This matters globally because East Asia is home to billions of people and crucial to world trade—any military escalation there could disrupt the global economy. The US has allied with Japan to counter Chinese influence, so Japan's defense decisions affect America's Asia strategy. When major powers increase military spending, it can trigger an arms race where countries keep building bigger militaries to feel secure.

world-news.title: 'Before, the land sustained us': Who benefits from Guinea's bauxite wealth?
world-news.summary: Guinea, a West African nation, has enormous reserves of bauxite, the raw material used to make aluminum, but ordinary citizens remain trapped in poverty despite this natural wealth. This is a classic example of "resource curse"—when a country has valuable natural resources but fails to distribute the profits fairly, leaving most people poor while corrupt leaders and foreign companies get rich. Bauxite is crucial for global industries including cars, airplanes, and electronics, making Guinea important to the world economy. The story illustrates how poor countries can be exploited: foreign mining companies extract resources with minimal benefits to local people, who often face environmental damage and displacement. This matters because it affects global inequality and shows why developing nations struggle economically despite having valuable resources—a pattern repeated across Africa and the developing world.

world-news.title: Huge blast kills dozens in rebel-held village in Myanmar
world-news.summary: A massive explosion in a rebel-controlled village in Myanmar, near the Chinese border, has killed dozens of people, with insurgents claiming explosives used for mining operations caused the blast. Myanmar has been in chaos since 2021 when the military seized power in a coup, overthrowing the elected government, leading to armed resistance from various rebel groups. This explosion highlights the ongoing civil conflict destabilizing the country and creating humanitarian crises. The incident matters globally because Myanmar sits strategically between China and Southeast Asia, and its instability affects regional security and refugee flows to neighboring countries. When countries descend into civil war with numerous armed groups competing for control, civilian populations suffer immensely, and it becomes difficult for the international community to maintain stability in the region.

world-news.title: Nicaragua confirms death in custody of Indigenous leader Brooklyn Rivera
world-news.summary: Brooklyn Rivera, a 73-year-old Indigenous rights leader, has died after spending nearly three years imprisoned in Nicaragua under the authoritarian government of President Daniel Ortega. Rivera was detained without proper legal process—what's called "arbitrary detention"—and held in isolation, raising serious concerns about human rights abuses. This case exemplifies how some governments suppress political opponents and Indigenous leaders who challenge their authority. International human rights organizations have condemned the death and the government's treatment of Rivera. It matters globally because Nicaragua's government is increasingly authoritarian, violating basic human rights protections, and the situation reflects a broader troubling trend in Latin America where some leaders are consolidating power and silencing critics through imprisonment and intimidation.

world-news.title: Donated milk reaches Cuba amid deepening shortages
world-news.summary: Cuba has begun receiving donated powdered milk from Mexico and Uruguay as the island nation faces severe shortages of basic goods including food and medicine. These shortages stem from Cuba's economic crisis, caused by decades of US economic sanctions (restrictions on trade and financial dealings), outdated industries, and mismanagement. The need for international aid highlights how Cuba's isolation from the global economy has devastated living standards for ordinary Cubans. This matters because it shows how geopolitical conflicts between nations—particularly the long US-Cuba hostility dating back to the 1960s—directly harm ordinary people's ability to get food. The situation also reflects broader economic problems: when countries are cut off from world trade through sanctions, their economies struggle to produce goods, and citizens suffer shortages. Cuba's crisis influences regional migration patterns, with people fleeing to neighboring countries.

science-space.title: NASA’s X-59 Prepares for First Supersonic Flight
science-space.summary: NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft is preparing for some of its most significant flights yet. The X-plane is about to begin a new block of test flights that will include its first time flying faster than the speed of sound and other mission-critical objectives. “What comes next is the first time this one-of-a-kind aircraft […]

science-space.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Daniel Stubbs, NASA aerospace engineer: If you’ve driven through a cloud of dust and dirt that temporarily obscured your view, you’ve gotten a partial picture of a potential problem that NASA’s human landing systems for Artemis will face when they land on the Moon. Daniel Stubbs, an aerospace engineer […]

science-space.title: NASA Uses Mineralogical Marker to Understand Ancient Martian Climate
science-space.summary: Scientists analyzed 20 Martian samples collected by NASA’s Curiosity Rover and found that differences in hematite crystallite size at varying elevations could serve as a new mineralogical marker for understanding Mars’ ancient climate.

science-space.title: 'The Arrival' at 30: Charlie Sheen’s criminally underrated alien invasion thriller feels much scarier today
science-space.summary: 'If you can't tend to your own planet, none of you deserve to live here.'

science-space.title: Watch this bio-inspired Mars rover concept 'swim' through sand on curved wheels (video)
science-space.summary: The sandfish locomotion idea has been adopted from Scincus scincus, a lizard found in the Sahara desert.

science-space.title: The 11 best cozy sci-fi games for those chill cosmic vibes
science-space.summary: From farming and walking sims to space trucking, you can live that cozy life… but in space!

science-space.title: Can solar sails really send humans out into interstellar space?
science-space.summary: "I think these are not far-out type of ideas; they are not really futuristic ideas that we are talking about."

science-space.title: 'For All Mankind' star Wrenn Schmidt on her character's journey from NASA's mission control to the slammer (interview)
science-space.summary: ‘She listens to Dolly Parton now. She’s exposed to all kinds of things being in jail, because she’s got nothing but time.’

science-space.title: This Week In Space podcast: Episode 212 — Kabooms, Starship, and a Moon Base
science-space.summary: On Episode 212 of This Week In Space, Rod Pyle and Tariq Malik are joined by Space.com's Mike Wall to discuss the loss of Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket.

tech-ai.title: 'This is fine' artist KC Green reaches agreement with AI startup Artisan
tech-ai.summary: KC Green, the artist behind the famous 'This is fine' meme—that hilarious cartoon dog sitting in a burning room—had a dispute with an AI company called Artisan. The startup was using his artwork in advertisements without permission to promote their AI tools. This matters because it highlights a huge ongoing problem: as AI companies grow, they're scraping artists' work from the internet to train their systems, often without asking or paying the creators. Green's deal with Artisan shows artists are finally fighting back and winning. This is important to you because it sets a precedent—if you create content online (TikToks, art, music), AI companies can't just steal it anymore. The agreement means Artisan took down the ads and likely worked out some kind of compensation or licensing deal.

tech-ai.title: Black founders raise highest amount of quarterly funding since 2022, but there's a catch
tech-ai.summary: Black entrepreneurs who start tech companies just hit a major milestone—they raised the most money from investors in a single quarter since 2022. That sounds great, but there's a problem hiding underneath. According to Crunchbase research, Black founders still struggle to get initial funding because they lack access to the right networks and connections. Think of it like this: if you want to start a business, you need money from investors, but those investors often only fund people they already know or who know someone they trust. Black founders are locked out of many of those informal networks. This matters because diversity in tech leadership means better products for everyone, and it means billions in lost opportunities for innovation. The real story here is that one good quarter doesn't fix systemic inequality—the gaps in networking and early connections still need to be addressed.

tech-ai.title: Apple's strategy for smart glasses is the same as smart watches
tech-ai.summary: Apple is planning to release smart glasses—basically glasses with computer technology built in—and they're planning to compete with Meta and Samsung, but that's only half the story. According to Bloomberg reporter Mark Gurman, Apple's real target includes traditional eyewear companies like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Warby Parker. This is Apple's classic playbook: they don't just create a new category, they try to replace an entire industry. When Apple made the Watch, they weren't just competing with other smartwatches—they were trying to make traditional watches obsolete. Now they're doing the same thing with glasses. Smart glasses would let you see notifications, take photos, or get directions without pulling out your phone. This matters because if Apple succeeds, the way we interact with technology could fundamentally change. Instead of staring at screens, information would be overlaid on the world around you.

tech-ai.title: AMD's new pitch: our old tech is so good you should just keep using it
tech-ai.summary: AMD, a major computer chip company, is making an unusual promise at Computex 2026, a huge tech conference in Taiwan: if you buy a computer with their AM5 socket (that's the slot where the processor connects to the motherboard), you won't need to upgrade it until 2030. Normally, tech companies want you to buy new parts constantly to make money. AMD is instead saying their current tech is so good that you can stick with it for years. This is huge for gamers and PC enthusiasts who build their own computers, because upgrading components is expensive. AMD is also relaunching some older chips, betting that performance improvements have plateaued enough that people don't need the latest stuff. This matters to you because it challenges the entire tech industry's throwaway culture—maybe you don't actually need the newest thing every year. It also means computer gaming and work could stay affordable and accessible for longer.

tech-ai.title: Dell is bringing back the XPS 13 as a MacBook Neo competitor — with a temporary discount to $599
tech-ai.summary: Dell just announced a new XPS 13 laptop targeting students, and it's priced to compete directly with Apple's new MacBook Neo. Here's the deal: students can get it for $599 as an introductory promotion until September (back-to-school time), then the regular price jumps to $699. This matters because both Apple and Dell are competing for the same market—students who need affordable but powerful computers. The MacBook Neo (Apple's budget laptop) starts at $599, so Dell is matching that price exactly. A laptop is one of the most important tools a teenager will own for school, so pricing wars actually affect what you can afford. The XPS 13 is coming in July with modern processors (the brains of the computer) and the specs (capabilities) that rival more expensive models. This competition is good for students because it means companies are fighting to offer better value, which drives prices down and quality up.

tech-ai.title: Erin Brockovich takes aim at data center secrecy
tech-ai.summary: Erin Brockovich—famous environmental activist who exposed corporate water contamination and won a massive lawsuit—is now targeting data center companies and their lack of transparency. Data centers are enormous facilities filled with servers (computers that store and process information) that power everything from social media to AI. They use massive amounts of water for cooling and generate tons of carbon emissions. The problem is that these companies often don't disclose environmental impact details to the public. Brockovich wants them to be honest about how much water they're using, where it comes from, and what pollution they're creating. This matters because tech companies are expanding data centers rapidly to support AI and cloud services, and nobody's really checking whether it's destroying local water supplies or harming communities. If companies don't have to report their environmental damage, they have no incentive to reduce it. Brockovich is pushing for accountability and transparency.

tech-ai.title: The QD-OLED gaming monitor that started it all got a big upgrade
tech-ai.summary: Alienware, a gaming monitor company owned by Dell, is announcing an upgraded version of their groundbreaking first QD-OLED gaming monitor from 2022. QD-OLED stands for Quantum Dot OLED, which is fancy technology that makes screens incredibly bright, colorful, and sharp with perfect blacks. This new version, coming in July, has a refreshed design and enhanced features that make it even better for gaming. Why does this matter? For serious gamers, a monitor can make or break your experience—you could miss an enemy or lose a competitive advantage if your screen has even slight motion blur or poor color accuracy. QD-OLED technology eliminated a problem that plagued gaming monitors: ghosting (where moving objects appear blurry). This upgrade shows how the gaming industry is constantly pushing display technology forward. If you're a hardcore gamer or content creator who edits videos, monitor quality directly impacts your performance and how good your work looks.

tech-ai.title: Making sense of the debate over AI psychosis
tech-ai.summary: On a tech podcast called Equity, hosts debated whether tech CEOs are uniquely prone to 'AI psychosis'—basically, whether powerful tech leaders are losing touch with reality when it comes to AI's capabilities and dangers. 'Psychosis' usually means a mental condition where someone can't distinguish between real and imaginary, but here it's being used more loosely to describe how some CEOs hype up AI as a magical solution while ignoring real risks and limitations. This matters because these executives make huge decisions about AI development and deployment that affect millions of people. If they're genuinely disconnected from reality about what AI can and can't do, that's dangerous. Some CEOs predict AI will solve every human problem, while critics say they're downplaying risks like bias, job loss, and misuse. The debate highlights a real tension in tech: how do you balance optimism and innovation with realistic assessment of problems? Understanding how leaders think about technology helps you evaluate whether their promises are credible.

tech-ai.title: United flight forced to turn around because of a Bluetooth speaker name
tech-ai.summary: A United Airlines flight from Newark to Spain had to turn around and return to the airport just one hour after takeoff because of a security scare involving a Bluetooth speaker. According to passengers on Reddit, the crew repeatedly asked people to turn off their Bluetooth devices, gave a one-minute warning that two devices were still active, and then diverted the entire plane. Apparently, one Bluetooth speaker had a name that triggered security concerns—possibly something that sounded like a threat or contained suspicious keywords. This is funny but also kind of alarming: an entire plane full of people had to waste hours and fuel because of a device's name. It shows how overly sensitive security systems can be and how a simple mistake (like naming your speaker something edgy as a joke) can cause real disruption. This matters because it highlights the gap between automated security systems and common sense. It also raises questions: how much security theater are we actually getting, and at what cost?

tech-ai.title: This extravagant gaming laptop could ruin other screens for you
tech-ai.summary: The Asus ROG Strix Scar 18 is an 18-inch gaming laptop that a reviewer tested and described as basically seeing 'the promised land' of screen quality. It's a gigantic (for a laptop), incredibly bright display with zero motion blur, perfect colors, and sharp details. The catch? It's extremely expensive. The reviewer says once you've experienced a screen this good, using regular monitors feels like downgrading. An 18-inch screen is basically a portable desktop—huge for a laptop. This matters because displays are getting so good that they're becoming a real dividing line between premium and budget tech. If you're a gamer, streamer, video editor, or anyone who spends hours looking at a screen, display quality directly impacts eye strain, your work quality, and how enjoyable your experience is. This laptop represents the bleeding edge of what's possible, showing that the tech industry is still finding ways to improve the basics—like making things brighter and sharper. It's a reminder that sometimes specs (brightness, refresh rate) matter more than raw processing power.

energy-climate.summary: The renewable energy sector is entering a more difficult industrial phase – critical minerals are becoming a defining constraint on delivery.

energy-climate.summary: Masdar and Sungrow entered into an agreement for the supply of ESS and PV inverter technologies to support a gigascale RTC renewable project.

energy-climate.summary: Singapore’s NEA has appointed AECOM, Binnies and Ramboll JV to provide multi-disciplinary consultancy services for the phase 2 of IWMF.

school-education.title: Summer camp is child care, but many families miss out
school-education.summary:
When Glennys Torres was looking for summer child care for her kids, she envisioned a program full of outdoor play and time spent with friends.  But Torres quickly realized she didn’t have many options because of the cost. Each summer, she ended up enrolling them in a free school-based summer camp, but she said her […]

Students all over North Texas were planning a walkout, and the teen was eager to participate. […]

school-education.title: As farming goes high tech, universities grow new types of agriculture degrees
school-education.summary:
LINCOLN, Neb. — From the air, the spring landscape here is a vast expanse of brown farmland stretching out below, ready for farmers to dig in and plant their corn and soybeans, the state’s two top crops. But what flying above Nebraska doesn’t show is the vast network of aquifers and groundwater that supplies those […]

school-education.title: Four takeaways from our investigation into Texas’ disciplinary schools
school-education.summary:
Every year, Texas sends more than 100,000 students to its system of disciplinary schools, known as disciplinary alternative education programs. When the state legislature established DAEPs in 1995, during rising national concern about school violence, they were designed as a step up from a suspension for students who’d committed serious offenses.  But since then, the […]
The post Four takeaways from our investigation into Texas’ disciplinary schools  appeared first on The Hechinger Report.

school-education.title: Inside the latest global research on school cellphone bans
school-education.summary:
In the past decade, student achievement has stagnated or declined around the world as cellphones have become nearly ubiquitous Gen Z and Gen Alpha accessories. Educators from Florida to Sweden to Rio de Janeiro are responding with an increasingly popular tactic: restricting or banning cellphone use during the school day. But the first wave of […]

asia-pacific.title: Jakarta deploys troops against muggers, stirring dark memories
asia-pacific.summary:
Jakarta residents have largely welcomed a new police-military campaign against violent street crime, after a wave of brazen motorbike muggings struck fear into Indonesia’s capital.
But rights advocates warn that deploying soldiers alongside police blurs a line between law enforcement and military power, reviving memories of extrajudicial killings in a country still haunted by its authoritarian past.
On May 15, Jakarta police announced the formation of a special, 24-hour joint patrol task force..

asia-pacific.title: Beyond Nvidia: how US export curbs are forcing China to redesign its AI chip industry
asia-pacific.summary:
Under the weight of sustained US export controls on advanced semiconductors, China’s AI chipmakers are battling to forge a self-reliant silicon ecosystem capable of breaking Nvidia’s stranglehold on the market.
At the centre of this rivalry is a fundamental design debate: Should the country rely on the versatile graphics processing unit (GPU) or pivot to the highly specialised application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC)?
The fight is no longer about finding a single Nvidia clone; it is about.

asia-pacific.title: Australia catches Europe’s right-wing populist wave
asia-pacific.summary:
Australia’s populist One Nation party surged past the ruling Labor party to lead a nationwide opinion poll for the first time, highlighting voter disappointment with last month’s budget and reinforcing signs of a fracturing of the conservative side of politics.
One Nation’s primary vote advanced 4 percentage points to 31 per cent from a previous poll conducted ahead of the May 12 budget, while the centre-left government slipped 3 points to 28 per cent, according to a Redbridge Group/Accent...

asia-pacific.title: Who benefits from the ‘significant improvement’ in Hong Kong’s finances?
asia-pacific.summary:
It’s that time of the year again – tax season, so don’t forget to fill out your return. It’s also the time of the year when the Hong Kong government decides on pay rises for its civil service staff of over 170,000.
There’s a well-established system for deciding on pay rises. It includes the pay trend survey submitted to the government last week. Its preliminary results indicate that civil servants are in line for wage increases of 4.12 per cent for senior civil servants, 2.64 per cent for...

asia-pacific.title: Parents begin wealth transfer plans when children are as young as four
asia-pacific.summary:
[The content of this article has been produced by our advertising partner.]
Affluent parents in Hong Kong and mainland China have begun wealth transfer planning when their children are as young as four, according to a new survey by DBS Hong Kong.

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong’s John Lee on Central Asia mission to explore new markets, expand ties
asia-pacific.summary:
Hong Kong’s leader begins an official visit to Central Asia on Monday with his largest delegation to date, aiming to explore new markets amid geopolitical uncertainties and help mainland Chinese companies “go global”.
Sources said dozens of memorandums of understanding (MOUs) were expected to be signed by representatives from various sectors and their counterparts in Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan during the five-day visit.
One of the deals under the spotlight will be between tungsten miner Jiaxin...

asia-pacific.title: Why a fabled Chinese surgeon’s tomb may help rewrite history of anaesthetic use
asia-pacific.summary:
On October 16, 1846, the American dentist William T.G. Morton successfully demonstrated the use of inhaled ether anaesthesia at Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston, an event widely considered a turning point in modern surgery.
But this record may have to be rewritten after new evidence emerged that in the 14th century AD, Chinese surgeons were making their own anaesthetics from plants.
Their use had previously been recorded in ancient Chinese texts, but now the first physical evidence...

asia-pacific.title: Japan Buddhist monk bar has heaven- and hell-inspired drinks, offers beating service
asia-pacific.summary:
A Tokyo bar run by Buddhist monks has become an online sensation for its unusual blend of cocktails, chanting and death-themed rituals.
At the Vowz Bar in the city’s Shinjuku district, patrons can sip drinks inspired by heaven and hell, seek life advice from the monk staff and listen to sutras performed in a modern musical style.
According to a Chinese visitor @kaolababyy, the menu features cocktails such as “Nirvana in the Pure Land”, “Never Ending Suffering in Hell” and “Love and Hate leading.

asia-pacific.title: Myanmar’s China-backed mega dam revival risks Kachin rebel backlash
asia-pacific.summary:
Deep in the forested highlands of northern Myanmar, where the Irrawaddy River rises from two tributaries in the hills of Kachin state, a contentious infrastructure project is stirring again – and threatening to reopen wounds that never fully healed.
The Myitsone Dam, a US$3.6 billion Chinese-financed hydroelectric megaproject that was shelved more than a decade ago after igniting a storm of popular fury, is back on the table.
Myanmar’s military rulers have begun holding public consultations on..

asia-pacific.title: Chinese EV makers shift focus from price wars to AI capability: Morgan Stanley
asia-pacific.summary:
The focus of competition for Chinese electric vehicles (EVs) is shifting from prices to artificial intelligence capability, as carmakers try to manage weakening demand amid tightened regulations, according to Morgan Stanley.
The push in AI would likely bring models with conditionally autonomous driving capability, or Level 3 (L3), to market, said Tim Hsiao, head of the Greater China auto and shared mobility research team at Morgan Stanley, in a recent interview with the South China Morning Post.
